ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper presents a clear set of novel algorithmic components and their integration: truncated importance sampling with bias correction for marginal policy gradients (Sec. 3.2), a closed‑form, efficient trust‑region update using an averaged policy network (Sec. 3.3), and Stochastic Dueling Networks to estimate V and Q in continuous actions (Sec. 5.1). While it builds on existing pieces (Retrace, TRPO, importance sampling, replay), it (a) proves a theoretical connection rewriting Retrace via the truncation+correction trick (Sec. 7), and (b) combines these elements into the first scalable, stable off‑policy actor‑critic with replay (ACER) that works across discrete and continuous domains — a non‑trivial advancement that is likely to be new and informative to experts.

CLARITY.justification: Overall the paper is well organized (Intro → Background → Method → Experiments → Theory → Conclusion) and clearly states the main contributions early (ACER and its three key innovations in the Introduction). Notation and equations are mostly consistent and definitions (Q, V, A, ρ, Retrace, etc.) are provided in the Method/Background, and helpful pseudocode/algorithms are included. Presentation issues that prevent a top score: several apparent typesetting/OCR errors and repetitions (e.g. duplicated paragraphs near the end), a few minor notation slip-ups in the provided text (instances where ρ is written as concatenation π(at|xt) µ(at|xt) rather than as a ratio), and figures are referenced frequently but not present in the provided extract (so their effectiveness cannot be judged here).

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main claims are supported by both theoretical derivations (truncation+bias-correction decomposition in Eq.7–9 and Proposition 1 showing the B operator equals Retrace and is a contraction; Appendix C proof) and empirical results (Atari 57-game evaluation and MuJoCo continuous-control comparisons with ablations). The math appears coherent and the Retrace/importance‑truncation connection is derived correctly under the stated finite-state/action and ergodicity assumptions. Minor methodological concerns weaken conclusiveness: continuous-control results report the mean/std over the best 5 of 30 hyperparameter runs (potential selection bias) and no formal statistical tests or per-game breakdowns are given; the off‑policy gradient relies on the limiting distribution β (assumes ergodicity) which is not fully discussed. These issues do not invalidate the core technical contributions but warrant caution in interpreting empirical superiority.
